← World Gallery
Regions Artists Timeline All works

Americas

Pre-Columbian builders, painters, and potters — Mexico to the Andes.

Cities larger than any in contemporary Europe, pyramid complexes aligned to the stars, and some of the finest portrait pottery ever made — all built without iron, wheel, or draft animals.

Click any image to enlarge. Use ← → or close with Esc.

Before 1000 BCE

Cueva de las Manos
Patagonian hunter-gatherers · 7300–700 BCE · Argentina
A cave with hundreds of human hand stencils, mostly left hands — because the artists were holding the bone tube of pigment in the right, blowing around it.
Caral
Norte Chico · c. 2600 BCE · Peru
A city of six pyramids and sunken plazas older than the pyramids of Giza. Without pottery, ceramics, or writing, its builders sustained a monumental civilization on irrigation and anchovy fishing from Peru's central coast.
Vessel with Reclining Figure and Birds
Andes, north coast, Cupisnique style · 2200–200 BCE · Ceramic, pigment (cinnabar?)
Kneeling Figure
Mexico, Guerrero, Olmec · c. 1200–600 BCE · stone
Vessel with Deity Mask
Central Mexico (Las Bocas, Puebla?), Olmec, Formative Period · 1200–900 BCE · ceramic, traces of pigment
Carved Bowl
Mesoamerica, reportedly the Tepecoacuilco River Valley, Guerrero, Olmec style (1200-300 BC), Formative Period · 1200–300 BCE · stone (travertine)
Head
Mesoamerica, Mexico, Olmec · 1200–300 BCE · stone
Container
Peru, North Coast, Cupisnique style · 1200–200 BCE · steatite

1000 BCE – 0

Celt with Deity
Mesoamerica, Mexico, Olmec · 1000–300 BCE · stone
Miniature Stirrup Spout Vessel with Incised Hatched Motif
by Cupisnique
Peruvian North Coast · cupisnique · -1000–-200 · Ceramic
Stirrup Spout Vessel with Raised Appliques Covering the Surface
by Cupisnique
Peruvian North Coast · cupisnique · -1000–-200 · Ceramic
Lord of Las Limas
Olmec · c. 900 BCE · Mexico
A greenstone sculpture of an adolescent Olmec youth holding a were-jaguar infant on his lap — found complete by two children playing on a hill in Veracruz in 1965.
Chavín Lanzón
Chavín · c. 900 BCE · Peru
A 4.5-meter granite spear, carved with a fanged anthropomorphic deity, rising through the floor of a small chamber at the center of Chavín de Huántar. The Andean Pantocrator — still rooted in situ.

Peruvian North Coast · chavín horizon · -850–-750 · Ceramic and pigment
Olmec Colossal Heads
Olmec · 1200–400 BCE · Gulf coast, Mexico
Seventeen basalt heads, up to ten feet tall, each with a distinct face. Carved with stone tools and floated on rafts from the quarry, 60 miles away.
Olmec Celt-Pendant
Olmec · 900–600 BCE · Mexico
A polished jade celt — an axe-like form — incised with a were-jaguar mask. Olmec celts were worn as pendants but derived their form from the sharpened agricultural tools of the period.

Stirrup Spout Vessel with Raised Geometric Scroll Motif
by Chavín
Peruvian North Coast · chavín horizon · -600–-400 · Ceramic
Chavín de Huántar
Chavín · 900–200 BCE · Peru
A mountain temple with underground galleries and the Lanzón, a carved granite blade — Peru's oldest pan-Andean god, still in his original chamber.
Nazca Lines
Nazca culture · 500 BCE – 500 CE · Peru
Hummingbirds, monkeys, and spiders drawn at the scale of football fields by clearing pebbles from the desert floor. Visible only from the air.
Monte Albán
Zapotec · 500 BCE – 850 CE · Oaxaca
A leveled mountaintop city with one of the earliest writing systems in the Americas. Its danzantes reliefs show contorted, stripped captives.
Raimondi Stele
Chavín · c. 500 BCE · Peru
A two-meter granite slab carved with the Staff God, his elaborate headdress readable in more than one direction — right-side up he is a fanged deity, upside down a different snarling face emerges from the headdress. The ambiguity is programmatic.
Olmec Wrestler
Olmec · c. 1000–400 BCE · Mexico
A squat basalt figure of a bearded, bare-chested man in a fighting crouch. A rare free-standing Olmec sculpture — the colossal heads sit on the ground, but this one coils like it could stand up.
Figural Vessel

Tikal
Classic Maya · 400 BCE – 900 CE · Guatemala
Step pyramids rising above the rainforest canopy. A Maya superpower that swallowed rival cities until a long drought took it down.

Jalisco state · jalisco · -300–300 · Ceramic and pigment
Paracas Textile
Paracas · c. 300–200 BCE · Peru
Embroidered mummy bundles wrapped in layer after layer of cotton and camelid fiber — winged shamans, deities, severed heads — colors still bright after two millennia in the Paracas desert.
Palenque
Classic Maya · 226 BCE – 799 CE · Chiapas
A small Maya kingdom with exquisite stucco portraits and one of the most carefully decoded royal tombs in the Americas — that of Pakal the Great.
Spear Thrower

Cloth with Procession of Figures
Central Andes, South Coast, Nasca people · 170 BCE–70 CE (radiocarbon date, 95% probability) · cotton and pigment (field); camelid fiber (borders and fringe)
Urn Figure
Mexico, Oaxaca, Zapotec · c. 150 BCE–200 CE (thermoluminescence date, 60 BCE–642 CE) · earthenware
Teotihuacan
Pre-Aztec · 100 BCE – 550 CE · Mexico
A city of over 100,000 at its peak — larger than Rome at the same time. Already abandoned when the Aztecs found it and named it "the place where gods were born."
Paracas Mantle
Paracas · 300 BCE–200 CE · Peru
A full camelid-wool mantle embroidered with tiny figures in repeating rows — some holding trophy heads. Preserved whole in the desert bundles of the Paracas necropolis.

by Nayarit, Chinesco style
Nayarit state · nayarit · 1–300 · Ceramic and pigment
Nayarit House Model
West Mexico · 200 BCE–300 CE
A ceramic cross-section of a family compound — courtyards, musicians on the roof, a figure sleeping inside. Nayarit tomb models preserve an ethnographic snapshot of a culture with no written record.
Textile Fragment
Peru, Moche, north coast · c. 50–650 CE · cotton and camelid fiber
Textile Fragments
Peru, Moche, north coast · c. 50–650 CE · cotton and camelid fiber
Warrior Vessel
Peru, North Coast, Moche, Moche style (50-800) · c. 50–200 CE · earthenware with colored slips
Frog Ornament
Peru, Moche · 50–800 CE · shell, stone, gold
San Agustín Stone Figures
San Agustín culture · 100 BCE – 800 CE · Colombia
Over 500 monolithic statues of guardians, jaguar-men, and birth scenes, scattered across an Andean plateau. Who the carvers were, or why they stopped, remains unknown.
Calima Gold Pectoral
Calima · Colombia · 1–800 CE · hammered gold
A hammered-gold chest ornament worn across the collarbone at full width — Calima lords in the Cauca Valley dressed in pounded gold heavy enough to have been audible as they moved.
Nose Ornament with Human Head and Condors Attacking Humans
Peru, North Coast, Moche culture (50–800 CE), early Intermediate Period · c. 100–300 CE · gold alloy

Butterfly Nose Ornament
Central Mexico, Teotihuacán, Classic period · 150–200 CE · jadeite-albitite?
Seated Male Figure
Mexico, Jalisco, Zacatecas Style · c. 200 CE · earthenware with colored slips
Pyramid of the Sun
Teotihuacan · c. 200 CE · Mexico
The third-largest pyramid in the world by volume, built over a natural cave that was probably the origin myth's portal to the underworld. The builders of Teotihuacan are unknown — the city had fallen five centuries before the Aztec arrived to rename it.
Pyramid of the Moon
Teotihuacan · c. 200 CE · Mexico
Smaller than the Pyramid of the Sun but terminating the Avenue of the Dead in its shadow of Cerro Gordo. Archaeologists tunneling into it have found successive burial caches with jade figurines, obsidian blades, and sacrificed wolves.
Feathered Serpent Pyramid, Teotihuacan
Teotihuacan · 2nd c. CE · Mexico
Third-largest pyramid at the site, facade covered in 260 snake heads alternating with headdress gods — Quetzalcoatl avant la lettre. Built over a mass sacrificial burial of warriors.
Feline Vessel
Central Andes, North Coast, Moche people · 200–850 CE · ceramic
Big-Eared Brown Bat Vessel
Central Andes, North Coast, Moche people · 200–850 CE · ceramic, slip

Obsidian Core, Blade, and Curved Neckpiece
by Colima or Jalisco
Mexico · colima · 250–350 · Obsidian
Lord of Sipán Tomb
Moche · c. 290 CE · Peru
The intact grave of a Moche warrior-priest, draped in gold, silver, turquoise, and Spondylus. Discovered in 1987 moments ahead of looters.
Moche Portrait Vessel
Moche · 100 BCE–500 CE · Peru
A stirrup-spout ceramic of an individual man — scarred, aging, recognizably alive. Moche potters made dozens of portraits of the same ruler across his lifetime: a visual biography in clay.
Nazca Polychrome Vessel
Nazca · 100 BCE–800 CE · Peru
Thin-walled clay bowls painted with anthropomorphic fishermen, trophy heads, stylized creatures — the same visual vocabulary as the giant desert Lines, reduced to pot scale.
Nasca Trophy-Head Bowl
Nasca · 100–650 CE · Peru
A polychrome ceramic bowl ringed with rows of severed trophy heads — the Nasca kept actual embalmed heads on cords, carried into ritual. The bowls depicted what the rites required.
Nasca Gold Mouth Mask
Nasca · 100 BCE–700 CE · Peru
A hammered gold mask with tentacle-like projections — worn in the mouth or nostrils at ritual moments. Gold meant "the sweat of the sun" in Andean cosmology.
Figurine with Back Rack
Mexico, Maya, Jaina style · 300–900 CE · molded and modeled pottery with traces of pigment
Bowl Depicting a Female Figure with Shield and Darts Motifs

Pair of Ear Plugs with Face of Figure in Interior
by Teotihuacan
Mexico · teotihuacán · 300–750 · Ceramic with shell
Maya Censer with Seated King
Early Classic Maya · 300–400 CE
A ceramic censer with a seated king modeled on its lid, his body a hollow for burning copal incense. Smoke rose literally through the figure — embodying divine breath.
Funerary Urn
Mexico, Oaxaca, Zapotec · 350–500 CE · terracotta
Trumpet with Feline Face
by Ceramist unknown
Santa Valley · moche · 350–850 · Earthenware and pigment
Moche Culture
Moche · 100–700 CE · Northern Peru
Before the Inca, the Moche built painted pyramids and made portrait pots so individual you can tell one person from another across fifteen hundred years.
Nasca Male Effigy Vessel
Nasca · 100–650 CE · Peru
A stirrup-spout vessel shaped as a seated man — painted face, closed eyes, carrying a cup. Nasca pottery matched its fine textiles in color range, unique in pre-Columbian Peru.
Sinú Gold Bird Finial
Sinú (Zenú) · Colombia · 5th–11th c. · cast gold
A cast-gold finial shaped as a long-beaked bird — the Sinú crowned staffs and litter-poles with these, their lost-wax casting so refined the Spanish melted most of it down for bullion.
Bottle
Peru · ca. 400 - 700

Peru, South Coast, Paracas or Nasca? · 410–540 CE (radiocarbon date, 93% probability) · cotton; dye-patterned plain weave
Maya Whistling Vessel
Early Classic Maya · 400–500 CE
A ceramic vessel shaped as a seated figure — when you tip water out, air passing through a hidden chamber makes the figure whistle. Ritual technology in fired clay.
Vessel with Running Figures
Peru, North Coast, Moche style · 450–550 · earthenware with colored slips

Veracruz state · mesoamerican · 450–1000 · Ceramic and pigment

500 – 1000

Huaca de la Luna
Moche · c. 100–800 CE · Peru
A staggered adobe pyramid rebuilt in six successive phases, each burying the previous in a deliberate act of renewal. Inner walls were painted with giant Ai Apaec decapitator gods in red, white, yellow, and black — pigments that survived because each phase sealed the previous art beneath fresh bricks.
Gate of the Sun
Tiwanaku · c. 500 CE · Bolivia
A monolithic basalt gateway with a central "Staff God" figure carved above the doorway, attended by rays of forty winged figures. Broken into three pieces in antiquity; reassembled in situ.
Skull Vessel

Maya Mirror-Bearer

A kneeling court dwarf, arms raised to support a disk of pyrite mirror — the mirror-bearer, a standard position of the Maya court. Status made visible in the carrying.

Copán
Classic Maya · 400–820 CE · Honduras
A small Maya kingdom with the finest stone sculpture in the Americas. Stelae-portraits of kings carved nearly in the round.
Moche Mountain Sacrifice Bottle
Moche · 500–800 CE · Peru
A stirrup-spout ceramic modeled into a small mountain with figures being sacrificed at its peaks — a portable vision of the Andean concept that gods lived atop peaks and demanded offerings there.

by Made for Ju’n Chakaw Naahb Cha’n
Guatemala · maya · 658–667 · Ceramic and pigment
Sarcophagus Lid of Pakal
Classic Maya · 683 CE · Palenque
Carved limestone lid over the tomb of K'inich Janaab' Pakal inside the Temple of the Inscriptions. Pakal falls into the jaws of the underworld at the base of the world-tree — or, to von Däniken, drives a spaceship.
Front Face of a Stela (Free-standing Stone with Relief)
Mesoamerica, Guatemala, Department of the Petén, El Perú (also known as Waka'), Maya (250-900), Classic period (200-1000) · 692 · limestone
Calakmul
Classic Maya · 5th–8th c. · Campeche
One of the two great Maya superpowers (with Tikal). A kingdom of 50,000 in a rainforest, its two largest pyramids rise 45 meters above the canopy.

Stela
by Maker unknown
Campeche state · maya · 702 · Limestone
Yaxchilán Lintel 24
Classic Maya · 725 CE · Mexico
Lady Xoc draws a rope studded with thorns through her tongue; her husband Shield Jaguar holds a torch over her. A bloodletting ritual carved in limestone with breathtaking fineness.
Head of a Young Noble
Honduras, Copán, Structure 10L-22A, Maya style (250-900) · 746 · stone
Tiwanaku
Tiwanaku · 500–1000 CE · Bolivia
A highland capital on the shore of Lake Titicaca. Its Gate of the Sun — cut from a single stone — faces the point where the sun rises at equinox.
Pyramid of the Magician
Late Classic Maya · 600–900 CE · Uxmal
An oval-based pyramid whose legend says it was built in one night by a dwarf magician. Every stone is carved; the central doorway is a monster's open mouth.
Maya Polychrome Vessel
Late Classic Maya · 7th–9th c. CE
A cylindrical vase painted with court scenes, Underworld journeys, or god-dialogues in red-orange slips. Maya "codex-style" vases are lost books — texts that outlived the paper they were once copied onto.
Jaina Island Figurine
Late Classic Maya · 7th–9th c. CE
Small ceramic figures of warriors, weavers, ballplayers, elite women — excavated from graves on Jaina, a tiny mortuary island off the Campeche coast. Among the liveliest small sculpture the Americas produced.
Veracruz Disk
Classic Veracruz · 600–900 CE · Mexico
A slate disk with cinnabar-painted relief — probably a mirror-back, worn at the chest as regalia. The iconography mixes Maya imagery with local Veracruz scroll patterns.
Veracruz Fish Hacha
Classic Veracruz · 600–900 CE · Mexico
An "axe-form" (hacha) shaped as a fish-head — ballgame regalia worn in the belt. Traces of original plaster and pigment still cling; hachas were as much costume as sport gear.
Panel with Royal Woman
by K’in Lakam Chahk
Guatemala or Mexico, Usumacinta River region, Maya style · c. 795 · limestone
Ballplayer Figurine
by Huaxtec
Veracruz state · huastec · 750–1450 · Earthenware and pigment
Double Pendant in the Form of a Mythical Saurian with Tusks
by Coclé
Panama · coclé · 750–1250 · Gold with plaster restoration of boar tusks
Circular Nose Ornament Incised with Concentric Bands
by Coclé
Venado Beach · coclé · 750–1250 · Spondylus shell
Nose Ornament in the Form of a Turtle with C-shaped Body
by Coclé
Venado Beach · coclé · 750–1250 · Spondylus shell
Nose Ornament in the Form of an Long-Nosed Saurian with C-shaped Body
by Coclé
Venado Beach · coclé · 750–1250 · Spondylus shell
Vessel Depicting a Sacrificial Ceremony for a Royal Accession
by Late Classic Maya
Mexico · maya · 753 · Ceramic and pigment
Relief of Enthroned Ruler (Chakalte')
by Chakalte'
Classic Maya · 770 CE
A limestone wall relief of a seated king before an attendant, carved and signed by the sculptor Chakalte' — one of the few Maya artists whose name we know, in a culture where authorship was sometimes acknowledged.
Container in the form of a Sacrificer
Peru, Middle Horizon, Wari Culture · 770–890 (radiocarbon date, 95% probability) · wood and red pigment (cinnabar)
plug for the bottom of Container in the form of a Sacrificer
Peru, Middle Horizon, Wari Culture, 7th -11th century · 770–890 (radiocarbon date, 95% probability) · wood and red pigment (cinnabar)
Bonampak Murals
Classic Maya · c. 790 CE · Chiapas
Three rooms painted wall to ceiling: a royal presentation, a battle, a victory dance ending in the torture of captives. The most complete Maya narrative painting to survive.
Xochicalco
Epi-classic · c. 650–900 CE · Morelos, Mexico
A fortified mountaintop city that hosted what may have been an international calendar congress, depicted on the Pyramid of the Feathered Serpent's beautifully carved reliefs. An underground observatory tracks the sun through a vertical shaft at zenith passage.
Wari Tapestry Tunic
Wari · 7th–11th c. · Peru
A tightly woven alpaca-wool tunic covered in flattened, abstracted figures and geometric grids. Wari weavers worked at more than 120 weft threads per inch — finer than most European tapestry.
Wari Sacrificer Container
Wari · c. 800 CE · Andes
A carved wooden vessel of a Wari sacrificer — trophy head in hand, his own face echoing his victim's. The Wari empire stretched south from Ayacucho to Cuzco, preceding the Inca.
Fragment (Band)
by Provincial Wari
Peru · huari · 800–1100 · Cotton and wool (camelid)
Headband
by Provincial Wari
Peru · huari · 800–1100 · Cotton and wool (camelid)
Beaker
Peru, North Coast, Sicán · c. 800–1370 · hammered and embossed gold alloy
Monkey Finial
Peru, North Coast, Sicán or Chimú, 9th-15th Century · c. 800–1470 · hammered and embossed gold alloy
Figural Flask
Peru, North Coast, Lambayeque Valley, Sicán or Chimú, 9th-15th Century · c. 800–1470 · hammered and embossed gold alloy
Macaw
Peru, North Coast, Sicán or Chimú, 9th-15th Century · c. 800–1470 · gold alloy
Tapestry-woven Yoke from a Tunic
Central Andes, North Central Coast, Moche-Wari Style · 800–1200? · camelid fiber and cotton, tapestry weave
Band Fragment
by Possibly provincial Wari
Peru · Pre-Columbian · 800–1100
Uxmal
Puuc Maya · c. 850 CE · Yucatán
Puuc-style architecture at its best, with mosaic façades of thousands of perfectly cut limestone panels forming rain god masks. The Pyramid of the Magician has uniquely rounded sides and the locals say it was raised in a single night by a dwarf.
Disk
Central Andes, Lambayeque or Chimú people · 850–1460 · Silver
Plate with an Image of a Moth
by Huaxtec
Veracruz state · huastec · 850–1250 · Earthenware and pigment
Bowl with Textured Surface Decoration
by Possibly Ancestral Pueblo
New Mexico · ancestral pueblo (anasazi) · 850–1050 · Ceramic and pigment
Bowl with Textured Surface Decoration in Basketry-Like Pattern
by Possibly Ancestral Pueblo
New Mexico · ancestral pueblo (anasazi) · 850–1500 · Ceramic and pigment
Tripod Vessel Depicting Monkey Hunters and Traders
by Classic Maya
Honduras · maya · 850–950 · Ceramic and pigment
Pair of Tripods with Knotted Motif
by Classic Maya
Honduras · maya · 850–950 · Ceramic and pigment
Storage Jar with Horizontal Bands of Interlocking Scrolls
by Ancestral Pueblo
Southwest · ancestral pueblo (anasazi) · 875–1130 · Ceramic and pigment
Chichen Itza
Post-Classic Maya · 600–1200 CE · Yucatán
On the spring equinox, sunlight strikes the pyramid's stairway to form a serpent that seems to slither down the temple of Kukulkan.
El Caracol
Maya-Toltec · c. 900 CE · Chichen Itzá
A round astronomical observatory whose upper windows align with the extreme positions of Venus — a planet so important to the Maya that their calendars track it to the day. The ruined tower earned its name ('the snail') from its internal spiral staircase.
El Tajín
Classic Veracruz · c. 600–1200 CE · Mexico
Its 'Pyramid of the Niches' has exactly 365 recessed square niches, one for each day of the solar year. Seventeen ball courts punctuate the site — carved with scenes of ritual decapitation that followed the game's conclusion.
Ballgame Palma
Mexico, Gulf Coast, Classic Veracruz style (600-1100) · c. 900–1100 · stone
Darkware Vessel
Peru, North Coast, Chimú style (900-1470) · 900–1470 · black ware

by Ancestral Pueblo
Arizona · ancestral pueblo (anasazi) · 900–1500 · Ceramic and pigment
Quimbaya Goldwork
Quimbaya · 300–1550 CE · Colombia
Cast gold figures of caciques, poporos, and seated shamans. In 1892 a diplomat gave 122 of them to Spain — one of the largest pre-Columbian collections anywhere.
Veracruz Palma
Classic Veracruz · 800–1100 CE · Mexico
A tall, flat stone "palma" carved in relief with a figure — worn at the waist as part of Mesoamerican ballgame regalia, symbolic gear rather than literal protective equipment.
Jar with Horned Serpents and Interlocking, Hatched-and-Black Stepped Designs
by Ancestral Pueblo
New Mexico · ancestral pueblo (anasazi) · 950–1400 · Ceramic and pigment

1000 – 1500

Taos Pueblo
Tiwa · 1000+ years continuous · New Mexico
Adobe pueblos stacked five stories high. Continuously inhabited longer than any community in the U.S. — the family on the top floor climbs ladders up and down.
Tumi Ceremonial Knife
Lambayeque · 900–1100 · Northern Peru
A gold sacrificial knife topped with the long-eared god Naylamp, eyes inlaid with turquoise. Used for ritual decapitation; now a symbol of Peru on souvenir shelves everywhere.
El Castillo, Chichen Itzá
Maya-Toltec · c. 900–1200 CE · Yucatán
A step pyramid whose 91 steps on each of four sides plus the temple platform equal 365. On the spring and autumn equinoxes, the sun throws a serpent-shaped shadow slithering down the balustrade toward carved snake heads at the base.
Tula
Toltec · c. 900–1150 CE · Hidalgo, Mexico
Capital of the Toltec — probably the 'Tollan' the Aztec considered their civilized predecessors. Colossal warrior atlantes 4.6 meters tall, bristling with atlatl spear-throwers and pectorals of stylized butterflies, still stand atop Pyramid B.
Kuelap
Chachapoya · 6th–16th century · Peru
A cloud-forest citadel enclosed by twenty-meter limestone walls, sheltering over 400 round stone houses on a ridge 3,000 meters above sea level. The Chachapoya held out longer than the Inca against Spanish conquest thanks to sheer isolation.
Diquís Stone Spheres
Diquís · 600–1500 CE · Costa Rica
Over 300 near-perfect stone spheres up to 2.5 meters across, scattered across the Diquís delta. Made by pecking and polishing gabbro boulders — purpose still debated.
Head Cloth
Central Andes, central coast, Chancay people · 1000–1460s · Cotton

by Chancay
Peru · chancay · 1000–1476 · Over-twist cotton
Atlantean Figures of Tula
Toltec · 10th–12th c. · Mexico
Fifteen-foot basalt warriors in feathered headdresses on top of Pyramid B. They held up the roof of a temple that is long gone.
Chaco Canyon
Ancestral Pueblo · 850–1250 · New Mexico
A ceremonial center of "great houses" — multi-story sandstone buildings in a treeless canyon. Roads radiate out straight for sixty miles, aligned to the sky.
Pueblo Bonito
Ancestral Puebloan · 9th–12th century · Chaco Canyon
A 'great house' of six hundred rooms and forty ceremonial kivas arranged in a half-D around a central plaza. Its T-shaped doors align with lunar standstills; ancient roads radiate outward across the San Juan Basin for hundreds of kilometers.
Mimbres Bowl
Mimbres · New Mexico · c. 1000–1150 · painted earthenware
A black-on-white bowl buried with the dead, its base punctured to release the spirit. The Mimbres abandoned their valley around 1150 and the painting tradition stopped with them.
Serpent Mound
Fort Ancient or Adena · c. 1070 CE · Ohio
A quarter-mile-long effigy mound of a snake swallowing an egg. The head aligns with sunset at the summer solstice; the coils seem to encode lunar cycles.
Canyon de Chelly
Ancestral Puebloan · 350–1300 CE · Arizona
Red sandstone canyon with cliff-dweller ruins tucked into shadowed overhangs. Later a Navajo stronghold; Kit Carson's 1864 campaign forced the Long Walk from here.
Tairona Double-Bat Bowl
Tairona · 10th–16th c. · Colombia
A ceramic bowl with twin bat effigies on its rim — the Tairona, ancestors of today's Kogi, made dense zoomorphic pottery that read as sculpture more than tableware.
Tairona Bird Pendant
Tairona · 10th–16th c. · Colombia
A cast tumbaga (gold-copper alloy) bird pendant — Tairona metalwork was lost-wax cast and polished to a warm gold surface. Worn at the breast of chiefs.
Painted Wrapping for Mummy
Peru, Central Coast, Chancay, 12th-15th century · c.1100–1400 A.D. · plain cloth, painted; cotton
Male Figure with Modern Additions
Peru, Central Coast, Chancay people · 1100–1500 · Original elements include the head and tunic; added elements include the skirt, red fringe, and belt; Cotton, camelid fiber, reeds; Slit tapestry (tunic and face); plain weave with supplementary weft (skirt)

by Chimú
Peruvian North Coast · chimú · 1100–1470 · Ceramic
Dresden Codex
Late Post-Classic Maya · 11th–12th c.
One of only four surviving Maya codices — 39 sheets of bark paper folded like a screen. Astronomical tables of Venus cycles, eclipses, and the rains. Almost everything else was burned by Spanish friars.
Water Jar (Olla)
Southwest, Ancestral Pueblo, Tularosa Black-on-White style · 1150–1325 · Ceramic, slip
Storage Jar (Olla)
by Ancestral Pueblo
Arizona · ancestral pueblo (anasazi) · 1150–1300 · Ceramic and pigment
Chan Chan
Chimú · 900–1470 CE · Peru
The largest adobe city in the Americas. Twelve miles of mud-brick walls carved with rows of seabirds, fish, and waves.
Backrest of a Litter
Central Andes, North Coast, Chimú people, late Intermediate period · 1185–1275 (radiocarbon date, 95.4% probability) · mixed media: wood, gold alloy, pigment, shell inlay
Mesa Verde Cliff Palace
Ancestral Pueblo · 12th–13th c. · Colorado
Villages tucked under sandstone overhangs. The Ancestral Puebloans built, lived in, and abandoned them in a little over a century.
Cahokia Mounds
Mississippian · 1050–1350 · Illinois
The largest city in North America before Europeans arrived — bigger than London in its day. Monks Mound alone is a hundred feet tall, built of earth by hand.
Chimor
Chimú · 900–1470 CE · Northern Peru
Kingdom that ruled the Peruvian coast before the Inca conquered it. Its capital Chan Chan is the largest adobe city ever built; gold, silver, and feather work survive in elite tombs.

Figurine Bell
Mexico, Mixtec, 13th-16th century · c. 1200–1519 · gold
Miniature Bowl with Interior Bird-Wing Motif
by Ancestral Pueblo
Southwest · ancestral pueblo (anasazi) · 1250–1400 · Ceramic and pigment
Fragment (Loincloth)
by Chimú
Peru · chimú · 1250–1470 · Cotton and wool (camelid)
Loincloth Panel
by Chimú
Peru · chimú · 1250–1470 · Cotton and camelid wool
Paquimé (Casas Grandes)
Mogollon · 13th–14th c. · Chihuahua, Mexico
Multi-story adobe pueblos north of the Maya world but connected to them — Paquimé traded in parrots, copper bells, and turquoise. Burned and abandoned before the Spanish arrived.
Muisca Raft (El Dorado)
Muisca · 600–1600 CE · Colombia
A miniature gold raft showing the chief and his attendants during the initiation ritual in Lake Guatavita — the story that grew into the European myth of El Dorado.
Tulum
Late Post-classic Maya · c. 1200–1500 CE · Yucatán
A small Maya port perched on twelve-meter cliffs above the Caribbean, walled on three landward sides. The Temple of the Descending God shows an inverted deity plunging head-first — probably the bee god so central to Yucatán cosmology.
Moundville
Mississippian · 1000–1450 CE · Alabama
A 300-acre ceremonial center with twenty-nine earthen platform mounds around a plaza, once home to about a thousand people. Moundville engravers produced copper plates of the Birdman and hand-and-eye motifs that defined the Southeastern Ceremonial Complex.
Chimú Gold Collar
Chimú · 1000–1470 CE · Peru
A broad gold collar from the kingdom of Chan Chan — the Chimú worked more gold than anyone in the pre-Columbian Americas. Much was melted down after the Spanish conquest; a fraction survives.
Aztec Tlaloc
Aztec · Central Mexico · 13th–16th c. · stone
The rain god with his goggle eyes and fanged mouth — Tenochtitlán sat on a lake-surrounded island, and the Aztecs owed their two harvests a year to Tlaloc and his dwarf rain-helpers.
Pedestal Plate
Panama, Coclé, 14th-15th century · 1300s-1400s · earthenware
Bottle with Underwater Serpents
by Mississippian, Nodena red and white
Arkansas · mississippian · 1300–1500 · Earthenware and pigment
Ritual Cache
by Salado branch of the Mogollon
New Mexico · salado · 1300–1400 · Wood
Mother-and-Child Figurine
Mexico, Aztec · 1325–1521 · Ceramic, pigment
Feathered Serpent (Quetzalcoatl)
Central Mexico, Aztec or modern, Post-Classic Period or modern · 1325–1521 · stone
Female Figurine
Central Mexico, Aztec, Post-Classic Period · 1325–1519 · jadeite
Figurine
Mexico, Aztec · 1325–1521 · pottery
Head in Mouth of Snake
Central Mexico, Aztec, Post-Classic Period · 1325–1519 · greenstone
Monkey
Central Mexico, Tacuba, Aztec, Post-Classic Period · 1325–1519 · stone
Tripod Dish
Mexico, Aztec, 14th-16th century · 1325–1521 · terracotta
Casa Grande Ruins
Hohokam · c. 1350 · Arizona
A four-story "great house" of caliche mud in the Sonoran Desert — the largest prehistoric structure in the American Southwest. Aligned to solstice and lunar events; abandoned before the Spanish arrived.
Codex Zouche-Nuttall
Mixtec · 14th c. · Mexico
A painted deer-hide screenfold codex reading back-and-forth across panels — the genealogies of the Mixtec kingdom, Lord Eight Deer conquering his rivals. One of the few surviving pre-conquest Mesoamerican books.
Serpent Labret
Mexico, Oaxaca, Mixtec · 1350–1519 · rock crystal
Mitla
Late Zapotec · 1200–1500 CE · Oaxaca
Religious capital of the Zapotec after the fall of Monte Albán, whose façades are veneered in thousands of perfectly fitted stone mosaic pieces forming grecas — geometric frets without a single repeating unit in some panels.
Mixtec Gold of Tomb 7
Mixtec · 14th c. · Monte Albán
500+ pieces of filigree-cast gold — pectorals, jaw-ornaments, rings — found in a reused Zapotec tomb by Alfonso Caso in 1932. A glimpse of Mesoamerican goldwork largely melted down after the Conquest.
Andean Processional Cross
Colonial Andean · 14th–15th c. attributions contested · Ecuador
A silver processional cross worked with Inca-era techniques for a Catholic rite — indigenous silversmiths picking up imported iconography within an unbroken Andean metalworking tradition.
Aztec Maize Carrier
Aztec · 14th–16th c. · Central Mexico
A stone seated male clutching ears of corn — an agricultural offering made concrete, maize as proxy for the god who gave it. Smaller and more intimate than the great Templo Mayor sculptures.
Aztec Gold Warrior
Aztec · 14th–16th c. · Tetzcoco
A cast gold-silver-copper figure of an armed warrior. Most Aztec metalwork was melted down by the Spanish in 1521; lost-wax gold figures like this one are staggeringly rare.
Macuilxochitl (Aztec Seated Deity)
Aztec · 14th–16th c.
A volcanic-stone deity, possibly Macuilxochitl ("Five Flower"), god of music, games, and excess, red pigment still clinging to the folds of his kilt. One of many minor Aztec gods with specialized domains.
Inka Khipu (Fiber Recording Device)
Peru, Inca Period, 15th-16th Century · c. 1400–1532 · cords, knotted and twisted; cotton and wool
Seated Figurine
Peru, Inca, 15th-16th century · c. 1400–1540 · stone
Knife
Peru, Inca Culture, 15th-16th century · 1400s-1500s · bronze
Fragment
Peru, Inca, 1400-1532 AD · 1400–1532 · pottery
Four-Cornered Hat
Peru, South Coast, Ica Valley, Inca Culture, 15th-16th century · c. 1400–1532 · camelid fiber
Head of Xilonen, the Goddess of Young Maize
by Aztec
Tenochtitlan · aztec · 1400–1500 · Basalt
Flower-Shaped Ear Ornaments
by Aztec
Mexico · aztec · 1401–1533 · Ceramic and gold foil
Mosaic Disk with a Mythological and Historical Scene
by Mixtec
Oaxaca state · mixtec · 1401–1500 · Turquoise
Peruvian Textiles: Mantle or Hanging, Loincloth, Turban, Padded Hat, Band with Tassels
Peru, North Coast, Chimú style, 1200-1460s · 1405–1640 · cotton; plain weave, brocaded and complex alternating gauze with 3 or 5 shots of plain weave between gauze shots
Loincloth
Peru, North Coast, Chimú style, 1200–1460s · 1425–1500 (radiocarbon date, 92.3% probability) · cotton; plain weave, brocaded and complex alternating gauze with 5 shots of plain weave between gauze shots
Coatlicue
Mexica · late 15th c. · Tenochtitlan
"She of the Serpent Skirt" — the earth goddess, severed neck bleeding two snakes, wearing a necklace of hands and hearts. Found under Mexico City in 1790.
Aztec Sun Stone
Mexica · late 15th c. · Tenochtitlan
A basalt disk over three meters across, carving the five successive suns of Aztec cosmology. Buried under the Zócalo in Mexico City until 1790.
Sacsayhuamán
Inca · 15th c. · Cusco, Peru
A fortress-temple above Cusco built from stones weighing up to 200 tons, fitted so precisely a sheet of paper won't pass between them.
Machu Picchu
Inca · 15th c. · Peru
A royal estate on a knife-edged ridge. The stones of its walls are cut so precisely that no mortar holds them — and they have survived five centuries of earthquakes.
Coyolxāuhqui Stone
Mexica · late 15th c. · Tenochtitlan
A twelve-foot disk of the moon goddess, dismembered by her brother Huitzilopochtli. Found by electric-company workers in Mexico City in 1978 — it launched modern excavation of the Templo Mayor.
Ollantaytambo
Inca · 15th c. · Sacred Valley
Fortress and town at the end of the Sacred Valley. Its huge terrace walls are fitted together in the same mortarless style as Machu Picchu — and in 1537 its defenders held off Pizarro's cavalry from them.
Coricancha
Inca · c. 1200–1500 · Cuzco
The Inca 'Golden Enclosure,' a Sun temple whose walls were once sheathed in gold plate stripped by the Spanish for ransom. The surviving trapezoidal masonry — so precise the joints cannot be penetrated by a knife blade — now supports the colonial Church of Santo Domingo.
Moray Agricultural Terraces
Inca · 15th c. · Peru
Concentric circular terraces sunk into the earth — each ring a different microclimate, temperatures ranging 15°C between top and bottom. The Inca agricultural laboratory, where crops were tested for alpine adaptation.
Inca Miniature Llama
Inca · 15th c. · Peru
Small silver or gold llamas, hollow, hollow-cast — buried in the highest-altitude shrines of the empire as offerings. Each one meticulous to the ear-tassel despite being smaller than a finger.
Inca Checkerboard Tunic
Inca · 15th–16th c. · Peru
A full camelid-wool tunic in repeating black-and-white squares — the standard uniform of Inca military officers, recognizable at a distance on the battlefield.
Ica Fragmentary Mantle
Ica Valley · 15th c. · Peru
A textile fragment from the Ica Valley — the coastal successor culture to Paracas and Nasca, still producing complex figural weavings on the eve of Spanish arrival.
Minature Handled Bowl with Textile-like Design
by Inca
Southern Peru · inca · 1450–1532 · Ceramic and pigment
Miniature Single-Handled Jar with Textile-like Pattern

Cuzco · inca · 1450–1532 · Stone
Minitature Tray Depicting Suche Fish and Peppers
by Inca

Band with Tassels
Peru, North Coast, Chimú style, 1200-1460s · 1455–1625 (radiocarbon date, 95% probability) · cotton; weft-faced plain weave
Sleeved Tunic
Central Andes, central coast, Chancay people · 1460s–1532 · Cotton, camelid fiber
Padded Hat
Peru, North Coast, Chimú style, 1200-1460s · 1460–1625 (radiocarbon date, 95.4% probability) · cotton; plain weave, brocaded
Double-chambered Vessel with Figures and Camelid
Andes, Chimú-Inka, late 15th - mid 16th century · 1470–1532 (Thermoluminescence date, 1310–1610) · ceramic

Central Andes (Peru), Chimú-Inka style, Late Horizon period · 1470–1521 (Thermoluminescence date, 1315–1615) · ceramic
Feathered Tunic
by Chimú
Peru · chimú · 1470–1532 · Cotton
Vessel with Reclining Dog
Central Andes, North Coast, Chimú-Inka style · 1470–1532 · ceramic
Carrying Cloth
by Provincial Inca
Peru · inca · 1476–1532 · Double cloth; edges embroidered with wool yarn; braided straps at each corner ending in tassels
Mantle or Hanging
Peru, North Coast, Chimú style, 1200-1460s · 1480–1635 (radiocarbon date, 95.4% probability) · cotton; plain weave, brocaded and complex alternating gauze with 3 or 5 shots of plain weave between gauze shots
Turban
Peru, North Coast, Chimú style · 1485–1640 (radiocarbon date, 95.4% probability) · Cotton; plain weave, brocaded and complex alternating gauze with 5 shots of plain weave between gauze shots
Templo Mayor
Aztec · 1325–1521 · Tenochtitlan
Dual pyramid of Huitzilopochtli and Tlaloc, discovered beneath downtown Mexico City in 1978 when utility workers struck the Coyolxauhqui Stone. Seven construction phases climb one over the other inside the exposed ruin.

1500 – 1800

Hiawatha Belt
Haudenosaunee · c. 1500 · Northeast Woodlands
A wampum belt recording the founding of the Iroquois Confederacy: five nations joined by a central tree. Belts were both treaty and record — texts read by running fingers across the beads.
Tlaltecuhtli Monolith
Aztec · c. 1500 · Tenochtitlan
A pink andesite slab of the Aztec earth-goddess, mouth open, hair writhing with skulls — found in 2006 at Templo Mayor, face down in a ritual burial. 4 meters long, the largest Aztec monolith ever found.
Inca Half Tunic
Inca · 16th c. · Peru
Half of an Inca unku — tightly woven tapestry with tocapu (small geometric blocks) that encoded social status, ethnic affiliation, or administrative role in ways still imperfectly understood.
Nose Ring
Colombia, Quimbaya · before 1550 · gold
Coronation Stone of Moctezuma Xocoyotzin
by Maker unknown
Tenochtitlan · mexica (aztec) · 1503 · Basalt
Feather Headdress of Moctezuma
Aztec · c. 1515 · Mexico
Four hundred quetzal feathers and gold discs mounted on a fiber base — long enough to reach a man's heels. Sent from Moctezuma (or his successor) to Charles V, now held in Vienna; Mexican repatriation requests have not yet succeeded.
Fragment from the Topacu Waistband of a Tunic (Uncu)
by Colonial Inca
Peru · inca · 1532–1700 · Cotton and wool (camelid)
Fragment from the Shoulder of a Tunic (Uncu)
by Colonial Inca
Peru · inca · 1532–1700 · Cotton and wool (camelid)
Coca Bag
by Inca-Colonial
Peru · inca · 1532–1650 · Cotton and wool (camelid)
Armband Depicting Horse and Rider with Animals
by Inca
Peru · inca · 1532–1600 · Gold
Doll
Peru · pre 1533
Codex Mendoza
Early colonial Mexican · 1541–1542
Aztec tribute lists and imperial history commissioned for Charles V. Captured by French pirates en route to Spain, ended up in Oxford's Bodleian. The most detailed document we have of Aztec administration.
Navajo Weaving
Diné · 17th c. onward · American Southwest
Tightly woven wool rugs and wearing blankets — the weaver puts a deliberate flaw into each one, a ch'į́įdii line, so the spirit of the piece has a way out.
Cuzco School Painting
Colonial Andean · 17th–18th c. · Peru
Indigenous and mestizo painters trained by Spanish religious, painting Virgins in tiered skirts that double as mountains (Pachamama), archangels in lace collars carrying muskets. Baroque European form seeded in Andean soil.
Box
Mexico · 1700-1750

1800 onward

Basket with Stamped Decoration
Native North America, Woodlands, Algonquian or Haudenosaunee (Iroquois), Post-contact Period · early 1800s · ash? strips, pigment
Northwest Coast Totem Poles
Haida, Tlingit, Kwakwaka'wakw · 18th–20th c.
Cedar poles carved with family lineage, clan animals, and narrated stories. Each is a library — readable top to bottom by those who know the figures.
Haida Argillite Carving
Haida · 19th c. onward · Haida Gwaii
Black shale carvings only the Haida may quarry, developed as a trade art for European sailors. Ship figureheads, pipes, and eventually complex narrative panels.
Spoon
Native North America, Northwest Coast, Alaska, Tlingit · late 1800s–early 1900s · horn, copper

New Mexico · navajo · 1865–1885 · Wool
Crooked Beak of Heaven Mask
Kwakwaka'wakw · 19th c. · Pacific Northwest
A transformation mask from the Hamatsa winter dance. The outer "cannibal bird" beak opens with strings during the dance to reveal a human face within — the return of the initiate.

Cigarette or Needle Case
Arctic, Aleut, Attu, Post-Contact · c 1875– 1917 (source unknown) · plain twine, wild rye grass with crossed openwork, patterned twined start, intersecting layered spokes, and plaited finish; silk embroidery (catalogue card)
Tray
Northwest Coast, Tlingit, late 19th century · late 1800's · Spruce root, Beach grass; twined
Northwest Coast Feast Ladle
Northwest Coast · late 19th c.
A carved wooden ladle for serving communal food at potlatches — the handle a bird or sea-creature, its beak curling up over the bowl. Prestige measured in generosity.
Northwest Coast Mask
Northwest Coast · late 19th c.
A carved and painted wooden mask — clan crest or shamanic being. Northwest Coast masks often had inner and outer faces; the outer opened to reveal the inner through strings the dancer pulled.
Tlingit Chilkat Blanket
Tlingit · Northwest Coast · late 19th c. · cedar bark and mountain goat
A ceremonial dance robe of cedar bark and mountain-goat wool — Chilkat weaving draws its formline crest figures freehand rather than on a grid, almost unique among loom traditions.
Tlingit Totem Pole
Tlingit · Northwest Coast · c. 1880 · carved and painted cedar
A Tlingit cedar totem pole — not religion or writing but lineage heraldry, declaring a clan house's crest animals. Most surviving 19th-century poles were cut down and sold; few stand in place.

Ghost Dance Shirt
Lakota · 1890 · Great Plains
Hide shirts painted with stars and eagles, worn in the Ghost Dance — believed to turn aside bullets. Shirts were collected from the dead at Wounded Knee.

Hopi Katsina Doll
Hopi · 19th c. onward · American Southwest
Carved cottonwood figures given to Hopi children to teach them the 400+ spirit beings of the Katsinam. Collectors later prized them so much the carving style changed to please buyers.
Zuni Fetishes
A:shiwi (Zuni) · traditional · New Mexico
Small stone carvings of animals — bear, eagle, mountain lion — each holding a spirit and often wrapped with an offering bundle of turquoise or shell.

Prometheus (Pomona College)
by José Clemente Orozco
Mexican muralism · 1930
A towering Prometheus bringing fire into Frary Hall, Pomona College. The first significant Mexican mural commissioned in the United States — and the doorway for a wave of muralists who followed.
Detroit Industry Murals
by Diego Rivera
Mexican muralism · 1933
Twenty-seven fresco panels wrapping the inner court of the Detroit Institute of Arts — Rivera's homage to the Rouge auto factory, workers at the assembly line deified like Aztec labor gods.
Maria Martinez Blackware
by Maria Martinez
San Ildefonso Pueblo · 20th c. · New Mexico
Maria and her husband Julian revived and reinvented matte-on-black pueblo pottery. Their pots sit in every major American museum — and still on shelves at San Ildefonso.
Sandías (Tamayo)
by Rufino Tamayo
Mexican modernist · mid-20th c.
Slabs of crimson watermelon arranged as if for a sacrificial altar — Rufino Tamayo returned to the same subject for decades, turning a peasant still life into national emblem.
Dolls
by Chancay
Peru · chancay · 1950–1984 · Contemporary assemblage of materials from a variety of past cultures. Cotton and wool
Frida Kahlo in her garden
by Freund, Gisèle
Mexico · 1952
Inuit Art
Inuit · 20th c. contemporary · Arctic
Carved soapstone, serpentine, and whalebone figures of hunters, spirits, and animals. A tradition fused out of older ivory carving and 20th-century print cooperatives like Cape Dorset.
Old Woman Seated against Ancient Inca Wall in Market at Chinchero, Peru
by Bunzl, George
Peru · ca. 1965
Mexico 1968
Mexico · ca. 1968
Mexico
by Friedlander, Lee
Mexico · 1975
Raven and the First Men
by Bill Reid
Contemporary Haida · 1980
Bill Reid's 4-ton yellow cedar sculpture of Raven discovering the first humans huddled inside a clamshell. Haida cosmology carved in a museum rotunda; an emblem of Indigenous art's modern presence.
Spirit of Haida Gwaii
by Bill Reid
Haida · Bill Reid · 1986–1991
A bronze canoe filled with thirteen mythic figures paddling together — Raven, Bear, Eagle, Wolf, and others. Reid worked on it for five years while Parkinson's overtook him.
